original_reasoning: Arabic: fine fluffy hair/fiber; a type of aromatic herb (marw). Greek: ginger (the spice). Both involve fine fibrous plant material in the aromatic/textile domain. Plausible connection via plant fiber and aroma.
Arabic: السلكوت, a type of bird. Greek: gathered together, collected. Faint link — birds gather in flocks; σύλλεκτος could apply to a bird that congregates. Plausible but weak.

Arabic: السمرود, a tall person; also brownish complexion (samrā). Greek: emerald (the green gem). Both involve the color domain — Arabic refers to tawny/brown complexion while Greek refers to green gem. Weak but plausible color adjacency. More phonological than semantic.

Arabic: الشرعوف = a plant; also the outer sheath of a palm frond (the top covering). Greek: high-roofed, high-ceilinged. Both share the notion of something covering/roofing from above — the palm frond sheath as a natural roof analog. Plausible mafahim link.

Arabic: الطفرة = a leap upward; also cream that rises. Greek: τάφρος = ditch/trench/moat. Both involve movement related to a sunken or elevated ground feature — leaping over vs digging into the earth. Plausible spatial-motion connection: one leaps the trench.

Arabic: الغرقد = a large thorny shrub (Lycium, boxthorn/goji family); also called العوسج when large. Greek: ἀγριόκαρδον = gum arabic tree (Vachellia nilotica), also a thorny Acacia-type shrub. Both are thorny shrubs found in arid/semi-arid environments. Plausible shared plant-family semantic link.

Arabic فنيق = a prized male breeding camel (highly valued animal); also a place name near Medina. Greek φοινίκινος = of the date palm/phoenix tree. Shared domain: both terms relate to the prized resources of the Arab/Semitic world — the date palm and the prized camel are emblematic of the same cultural-geographic space. Possible loanword from Phoenician/Semitic φοῖνιξ.

Arabic القرناس: a nose-like protrusion on a mountain or cliff face (from قرن = horn/protrusion). Greek κορωνόπους = buck's horn plantain (Plantago coronopus) — named for its horn-shaped leaves (κορωνή = crown/beak + πούς = foot). Both involve horn/beak-shaped projections in their core imagery.
